I would assume our highly optimized GBP (which has been killer for us, bringing in organic leads by dominating map pack) and our city-specific service pages ([city] house cleaning service) are the reason.
Would you advise this as next steps: highlight some past cleaning projects, make an awesome blog, and double down on city/specific service pages ([city] [service type])
Dominating our area with GBP has been KILLER, hoping for similar results by dominating in search results below map pack.
eg. instead of just house cleaning service in Brownsville, house cleaning service in McAllen, etc… do deep cleaning service in Brownsville, apartment move out cleaning service in Brownsville, New house move-in cleaning service in Brownsville, and so forth  for each city… Just an idea

posts.title: 16 New Clients in 4 Days Everyone should be doing this!!
posts.content:
16 New Clients in 4 Days
Everyone should be doing this!! Most were free leads.
Mentioned this in the recent podcast I did - thought I’d share it again…
When someone Googles “House cleaning near me”, people will find you on Google for free - IF your Google Business Profile is set up + optimized.
Out of the 16 new clients in the last 4 days, almost all of them were from our Google Business Profile showing up first in our area…
Even though we’ve been in business for less than a year, AND there are other cleaning companies near us with more reviews…
Make sure to do it!!
EDIT: I spent 2 months watching all of Jackie Chou’s stuff on YouTube. If you want to learn - go there. I know many people who have been scammed for SEO, so don’t pay someone unless you really know your stuff. It’s the wild west out here.

posts.author: Jaclyn Fernandez
posts.title: A WARNING. A long read but an important reminder. I started out as a virtual, or, as they say "
posts.content:
A WARNING.  A long read but an important reminder.
I started out as a virtual, or, as they say "remote" cleaning biz 2 years ago with 1 independent contractor.  Now, I clean about 80% of all homes with this IC and I'm not "remote" at all.  I have been working CLOSELY with her, and considered her a friend, for about 1.5 Years.  I was planning on treating her to a spa/girls day when we "catch up" on our jobs.
I just received a text from a realtor client from a job completed last September . This Realtor hired us for a pre-listing clean of a vacant home of an elderly person that moved out of state for family to take care of him.
Long story short, my IC that I thought I could trust, and would have NEVER imagined to do anything remotely crazy as this ~ was arrested back in March for taking 3 checks in the home , and making them out to herself.  Each check over $4,000.
As the police just released a press release just late last week, the realtor reached out to me.
Realtor was fired by her client.  The Police dept never made me aware of this, so the realtor assumed that I knew.  All this time, of course, this IC is still working with me.  The text message from this realtor is so upset that I never reached out to her or refunded the cleaning fee.
Now what do I do ? How do I trust again ? I'm so shaken up as I just found this out about 1 hour ago. Clearly, I will be firing her first thing in the morning.  I don't even know HOW to present this to her  professionally, knowing that she could have destroyed my local little business.
(Realtor chose not to publicly make a post about us / Police press release does not have our cleaning biz name on it ).
I honestly do not know what to do next.
She has 21 recurring clients including a full schedule this week that I desperately help on, but I cannot trust anymore.
So as a warning, one thing I've learned today... As much as you THINK you know someone, this may not be the case. It can happen to anyone.  And now, I'm stuck with a full schedule of jobs that I KNOW I cannot do by myself.   I feel that I cannot even trust again... How am I supposed to hire someone else ?
With that said, if anyone knows of any great, trustworthy  IC's in Northern NJ, I need help. Even just temporary help.

comments.author: Del Cid Verónica
comments.text: Did you run a background check on her before hiring? If not, you need to start having an onbording process that includes background checks.

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: Friendly reminder ab marketing The best (and most successful) biz owners in the house cleaning
posts.content:
Friendly reminder ab marketing
The best (and most successful) biz owners in the house cleaning space that I have talked to all have the same thing in common when it comes to marketing...
They know their marketing ROI, per channel.
If your marketing is a prayer or a hopeful wish...
That's a MAJOR red flag.
Everyone I talk to about $30k/mo says something along the lines of:
SEO is killing for us. The free optimization stuff (I talk about this in my recent posts)  is amazing, backlinks/citations are worth it in the long run.
Facebook has a lower close rate, but because the cost per lead is so low - It works.
It's the same story - Google ads... Thumbtack... Yelp... Flyers...
The main point: No matter what marketing they are doing, they are TRACKING spend + results so that they are 100% confident that every dollar they spend on marketing is producing profit.
Genuinely, if you focused on this one aspect of the business - you would solve a lot of headaches...
Yes, there are other problems you will face in other areas (labor, logistics, leadership, etc)
but LEADS is easily solved by TRACKING!!!
Through tracking, you can see the bottleneck.
Is it your ads? You'll be able to see that and fix it.
Is it your close rate? You'll be able to see that and fix it.
Is it your sales process/follow-up? Same deal.
Our tracking looks like a Google sheet that gets automatically updated for EVERY LEAD that comes in, takes into account all marketing spend, and what leads turned into bookings...
Then I can see, per channel, CPL, CAC, Close Rate, etc...
If you're struggling to be profitable on the marketing/sales side, take this post and throw it into ChatGPT or Claude and ask it to help you build a system to track all marketing and sales...
$34k last month, numbers projected this month are $40k conservatively and $45k is our stretch goal.
We were at $6k ish 12 months ago.
THIS IS THE BEST TIME OF YEAR FOR CLEANING BUSINESSES!!! Grow big!! Go kill it you guys!

comments.author: Harvey Le
comments.text: Thank you for reminding me that I need to do this.

posts.author: Madeeha Anwar Chaudhry
posts.title: Hey everyone, I’d really appreciate your thoughts on this
posts.content:
Hey everyone, I’d really appreciate your thoughts on this.
About three weeks ago, we met a local realtor at a Chamber of Commerce event. The next day, he asked us to do a move-out cleaning for one of his properties. We sent him the quote, but he didn’t enter his credit card info to confirm. Since we had just met him through the Chamber and wanted to build a good relationship, we decided to go ahead and do the job anyway.
The property was honestly in very rough shape, and our team worked hard to make it spotless. We even left a small thank-you gift for supporting a small local business. Afterward, he mentioned he’d ask the landlord to pay the same day, but that never happened.
I’ve since followed up several times by text, voice note, and email — all with no response. We ran into him again at a Chamber event last week, and he casually said he remembered he owed the money and was “trying to get it.” Still nothing.
Today, he met my business partner at another Chamber event and again said, “I know I owe you,” but still hasn’t paid. He also mentioned being the Vice President of the Chamber, which makes this situation even more uncomfortable.
My partner feels we should let it go to avoid conflict, but I personally believe accountability matters - especially within a professional network like the Chamber. We’re a small, woman-owned cleaning business trying to build honest, local relationships and this kind of experience is disheartening.
So I’d love to hear your advice - what would you do in this situation? Should I formally report it to the Chamber, or just move on and take it as a learning experience?

posts.author: Tatiana Trunilina
posts.title: After a year of running a cleaning company, I understand these things
posts.content:
After a year of running a cleaning company, I understand these things:
Market is everything. Turns out if it's easy to hire in your market, it's extremely hard to sell your services. Oversupply of workers brings prices down in that area
The market is better where it's hard to hire. But you are struggling to find cleaners.
Things can be vastly different in different cities even within the same state
When people teach you how to open and run a cleaning business, their strategies work in their specific city and will probably not work in yours.
We are barely hanging on, servicing a handful of regular customers which we acquired during the summer peak season. We had to turn off all of our ads because they were spending more than we were making from them. We tried a number of B2B marketing strategies even in peak months with very poor results. Most other companies in my area are dealing with the same issues, and this has been going on since September.
Now I'm thinking if I should try to move to another market where the grass is greener. I hear lots of cleaning business owners in other parts of the US make multiple 6 figures a year, especially after a year in business, but in my specific market (Texas) cleaning companies are either closing or struggling just as we are.
Or maybe I should just go sell cookies at a farmer's market.

posts.author: Alina Kalinina
posts.title: Looking for some advice from those of you who do commercial walkthroughs
posts.content:
Looking for some advice from those of you who do commercial walkthroughs. We’ve been cold calling and setting up walkthroughs, but this week we ran into some issues.
We had two scheduled-one at a car dealership and another at a shipping facility. In both cases, the person we originally spoke to on the phone gave us a time, but when we showed up the managers on site weren’t aware or were too busy. At the dealership, the GM never told the floor manager, so we were asked to leave. At the shipping facility, our sales rep waited almost an hour, but the manager never came out, and all that happened was exchanging business cards and being told to call back.
For those of you with more experience:
How do you set up these walkthroughs so the right person is actually expecting you?
Do you confirm in a specific way to avoid this?
Or is this just part of the numbers game with cold calling, and sometimes you have to expect wasted time until you connect with someone serious?

posts.title: Hi all We use BookingKoala for our snow clearing business
posts.content:
Hi all
We use BookingKoala for our snow clearing business. During larger snow events, we subcontract a significant portion of the work rather than relying solely on in-house providers.
We are exploring whether a referral agency–style setup would be a better fit than maintaining providers internally and subcontracting overflow.
Does anyone here operate BK using a referral agency model, or something close to it, and would be open to sharing how that model is explained to customers on your website?
I am particularly interested in:
How the booking is positioned (request vs confirmed service)
How independent providers are described
How expectations and liability are framed for customers
Referral-style models are not common in our country, so I am looking to learn from those who have implemented this successfully elsewhere.
Appreciate any insight or examples you are willing to share.

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: 5 bookings today - so simple… Going under for deviated septum surgery tmr
posts.content:
5 bookings today - so simple…
Going under for deviated septum surgery tmr. I know the biz is in great hands
Praise God!!
1. Service - Amazing cleaning + customer service.
2. Marketing - Get your CPA under $100.
3. Sales - Use a high close rate script framework + KNOW your objection handling (we close above 40%, usually above 50%)
4. Systems - Make everything repeatable & scalable
5. Analytics - Track everything so you can see from a bird’s eye view and make overall course corrections!

posts.author: Camili Pro Maids Services
posts.title: “Does BookingKoala Plan to Integrate with Dialpad for SMS Notifications?” Hello BookingKoala Te
posts.content:
“Does BookingKoala Plan to Integrate with Dialpad for SMS Notifications?”
Hello BookingKoala Team,
I’ve been a long-time user of BookingKoala for our residential and commercial cleaning business, and one of the features I value is the built-in SMS notification system (which currently uses Twilio).
Given our operational needs (multi-location, large crews, high volume messaging, strict tracking of notifications for clients, staff and providers) we are always evaluating communication platforms for reliability, cost, compliance and workflow flexibility.
We are curious whether you have any plans to support or fully integrate Dialpad as an alternative SMS/MMS (or unified communications) provider instead of or in addition to Twilio. A few reasons why Dialpad is on our radar:
Dialpad offers SMS & MMS business text messaging built into its unified communications platform. Dialpad+1
Dialpad has open APIs and supports custom integrations and workflows. Dialpad+1
For our business model — high-volume client, staff, and provider notifications across multiple service zones (Houston, Katy, Cypress, Conroe, etc) — a platform that combines voice + messaging + integrations may offer operational efficiencies.
So our specific questions are:
Are there any current or upcoming BookingKoala roadmap items to natively support Dialpad (or any other SMS provider besides Twilio)?
If not, is there a developer/API workaround you’d recommend for connecting Dialpad via Zapier/Make/Integromat to BookingKoala notifications?
How does BookingKoala evaluate alternative SMS providers in terms of compliance (A2P messaging rules, registration requirements) which Dialpad highlights in their help docs?
From your perspective, what are the limitations or risks of moving away from Twilio (in your built-in setup) to a custom provider?
We value BookingKoala as a central platform for our staff scheduling, billing, client notifications, etc., and adding flexibility in our messaging stack could further help our scalability and brand reputation (especially for our premium cleaning service with strict SLA, live statuses, etc).
Thanks for your time and looking forward to your feedback.

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: WE DID IT!!!
posts.content:
WE DID IT!!!
AI has been going crazy lately… but figuring out how to actually use it in a cleaning business has been lowkey frustrating lol
We use it all the time in our real estate + SaaS businesses, so I kept feeling like I was missing something here for our house cleaning biz
Recently started messing with Claude + this thing called MCP (still couldn’t fully explain it if I tried
), and it finally started to click. It basically connects AI to your actual tools. CRM, booking software, sheets, messaging… etc. so it’s not just answering questions, it’s kind of watching what’s going on.
Our cleaning biz is run by VAs, so this has been surprisingly helpful just for staying on top of things and getting a vibe check on how things are. Stuff like:
* catching leads that never got a reply
* noticing which follow-ups people actually respond to
* double checking payroll vs bookings
* bringing old convos back up that got missed
* even listening to call transcripts and pointing out what went well / didn’t
It’s not perfect and I’m still figuring it out, but this is the first time AI has felt actually useful for this kind of business.
Curious if anyone else has found simple, real ways they’re using it? In your cleaning biz (or real estate or whatever biz you own)
Side note, I got really deep into this recently bc I realized one of our systems SMTP setting were goofed and we had 45 leads in the last two weeks that weren’t contacted (that nobody knew about) which was roughly $6-8k in first time bookings in the house cleaning biz we missed out on w our avg close rate… Don’t want to think ab the LTV lost
but that’s what put a fire under my butt to really figure out how to get ai involved here.

On July 29, I said in a post August was just shy of $10k…
We ended August at $15.5k. September is looking even better!
Started the biz in Feb… Need to pinch myself lol
Very grateful.
Couldn’t have gotten here without everyone’s help, esp Jacob Elijah. All love for you big dude
Also some love for Christopher Peterson Jacques H. Bastien Kirsten Boudreaux. Even though our conversations have been short, I’ve gleaned golden nuggets that have helped tremendously.
I really believe we’ll hit consistent +$30k months by Feb of 2026 with our determination. It’s a stretch, but will be worth it.
The 3 things that have contributed to our success most:
- My best friend and business partner. He’s irreplaceable. I wouldn’t want to do this without him, it’s been much more fun doing it together and we have strengths in different areas.
- Mentors and other business owners (like those above) are legit like magic sometimes. They say one thing that unlocks your brain and things just start to work because you get unstuck and try new things.
- Marketing. Marketing. Marketing. I wish I could say it 1000x. If you don’t have leads -> you don’t have sales -> you’re a dying cleaning biz. Dial in marketing (lead flow, automations, follow-up workflows, etc). Dial in sales. Take good care of your customers and cleaners.
July was $9.5k, August was $15.5k. Why? Marketing was dialed in and sales was dialed in.
TBH we’re still only like 80% of the way dialed in on marketing, and 80% on sales. Can’t wait to see what comes next
Will try to post here more, too busy in the biz lol

posts.author: Francis Clase
posts.title: Major updates coming to Google My Business and reviews
posts.content:
Major updates coming to Google My Business and reviews. This month looks like it's way more AI-driven:
Key Changes in Google Business Profile
Updated Review Policy Enforcement
• Stronger enforcement against review manipulation (no gated or incentive-based review requests).
• Reviews may be removed retroactively if flagged by AI or policy checks.
Increased Review Scrutiny
• AI now checks patterns across reviews and can remove low-value or suspicious content (even older reviews).
• Profiles might be restricted from receiving new reviews temporarily.
Email Alerts for Restrictions
• Google sends notifications when review restrictions are lifted or changed.
Focus on Complete & Accurate Profiles
• Having consistent Name/Address/Phone and updated hours, photos, services listed is more important than ever.
AI-Driven Content Features (rolling forward into 2026)
• AI-generated local answers and visual formats may start showing up in local search results (based on profile activity).
New Transparency Attributes (where supported)
• Profiles can add things like sustainability practices, accessibility, and service guarantees.
WHAT THIS MEANS (SUPER BRIEF)
Google is being more aggressive about cleaning up fake or low-quality reviews.
Business owners must follow neutral review request practices and avoid incentives or selective asks.
Profiles with complete, accurate info and consistent activity are rewarded, especially as AI features expand.

posts.author: Kirsten Boudreaux
posts.title: Let’s talk about core values, both personally and in business
posts.content:
Let’s talk about core values, both personally and in business.
I’ve realized I have a tendency to “lose the plot,” especially as my businesses have grown. So I’m taking a minute to slow down and reflect, and I’d love to hear from you too.
What are your top 3 personal core values, and your top 3 business core values?
(They can absolutely overlap.)
Here are mine:
Personal Values
1. Integrity – Be honest with yourself. Do the right thing when no one’s watching. Help others when you can.
2. Discipline – Follow the plan even when you don’t feel like it. Show up. Be consistent.
3. Authenticity – Be real. Enough said.
Business Values
1. Integrity – Lead with honesty and expect the same from the people around you.
2. Courage – Take calculated, meaningful risks that actually move the business forward. At the end of the day this is what being an entrepreneur is about!
3. Service – Lean into “losing yourself in the service of others.” Be compassionate. Go the extra mile without expecting anything back. Maybe it’s my belief in karma talking, but what goes around truly does come around, and in the cleaning industry, you see this more than you’d think.
Tag a friend, and let’s keep the conversation going!

VOTE:
https://feedback.bookingkoala.com/contribute/feature-requests/e/49846/
Hello BookingKoala Team,
I wanted to share a suggestion that could greatly improve conversions for companies like ours.
When we provide a quote over the phone, it would be extremely helpful if that quote information could automatically be added to the Abandoned Carts section within Automation Campaigns.
This would allow us to follow up with those customers through automated reminders, just like online bookings that were not completed.
Many customers receive quotes by phone but don’t finalize the booking right away, so having them included in abandoned cart workflows would help us recover more jobs and increase revenue.
This feature would make a significant difference in our day-to-day operations.
Thank you for your time and consideration!

posts.content:
Looking for advice from fellow cleaning business owners…
We’ve been working with a client who manages several Airbnb properties. From the beginning, we gave a clear price and asked them to set up automated payments with a card.
They made several excuses and insisted on paying by check.
After several cleanings, they paid less than the agreed amount, deducting part of the fee on their own without discussing it first.
My question is:
How do you handle these situations?
Do you accept checks or only card payments?
Do you ever make exceptions, or do you stick to your policy no matter what?
I’d love to hear how others protect their business when working with recurring clients.
Thanks for sharing your insights!

posts.author: Powers of Clean
posts.title: It has finally happened, we are turning profit!
posts.content:
It has finally happened, we are turning profit!
This has been 18 months in the making. I know, that sounds ridiculous... hear me out.
We don't use any automations, we don't use any AI, we manually take care of each and every person and have been overly hands on every step of the way. We have had times with helpers and times when we are only owner operated, BUT we have found an AMAZING helper to take the day time work while owners do night time commercial after the office day is done.
We started our company to give back to our struggling community. We charge little and we pay big. We have been nothing but blessed every step of the way. Truly grateful!!

comments.text: If you want to scale, ai and automations are key.

posts.title: Need some advice!
posts.content:
Need some advice!
Last year we tried CleanGenie but ended the subscription because we were simply too damn busy keeping up with demand — all of our growth has come from word of mouth and a full team of W-2 employees, not a single dollar spent on ads.
Now we’re looking to scale and step back from micromanaging by hiring a virtual assistant and implementing a solid CRM system to handle scheduling, follow-ups, and client management.
I’ve seen a lot of people still recommending GoHighLevel, but I’ve also heard good things about ZenMaid (i dont think its a white labeled gohighlevel though?). Curious what other cleaning business owners are using to automate and scale efficiently.

posts.author: Brunson Paul Sayes
posts.title: How do you handle when you have multiple cleaners with different booking criteria, such as
posts.content:
How do you handle when you have multiple cleaners with different booking criteria, such as:
• They can't clean a house with cats
• They can't clean larger homes by themselves
Right now, what we do is we have to manually go and check all this information before we can book a client, which causes a bunch of issues in the booking process. Also, I think it leads to a lower conversion rate because people are having to wait instead of just booking right away.
Does anybody have a way that this can be more automated so that way people that have special criteria can be booked without having all the manual work?

comments.author: Jacques H. Bastien
comments.text: Do you auto book your cleaners (without needing them to approve) or do you have to get approval from each?

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: ChatGPT… Edit my website Been cracking away at my website for a pretty long time, hyper-optimiz
posts.content:
ChatGPT… Edit my website
Been cracking away at my website for a pretty long time, hyper-optimizing it for SEO. Don’t wanna pay someone on Fiverr bc I don’t think they’ll do a good enough job optimizing, and don’t wanna pay $1000 to have someone who really knows what they’re doing do it.. Decided to learn.
But now it’s been monkey work the past week, and instead of just putting my head down and slaving away - I wrote a really really long prompt of what I want sone on each page… Now I’m watching as ChatGPT Agent does it. Pretty satisfied so far. Wild that this is where technology is. Pretty soon our home-bots will be cleaning for us.

posts.author: Elsie Ikpo Kalu
posts.title: Hi All , I might be getting something wrong here or totally on to something
posts.content:
Hi All
,
I might be getting something wrong here or totally on to something.
I was doing some website restructuring and the tech guy ended up deactivating my website and my URL now leads to an error page. Since I was traveling, I didn’t have the time to fix it immediately.
Then something unusual happened. I started receiving calls straight from Google. Very high intent, very qualified leads…these sound like referrals. I have booked an $8k per month commercial cleaning business, and just this morning have onboarded 2 recurrent cleans (there are more). I’ve asked them all how they found me and they said, they ‘just searched on Google’.
I have been terrified to restore my website. I’m doing nothing for SEO. I do have over 50 5star Google reviews, but I’ve always had those and NEVER seen this kind of results for the over one year we’ve been in business.
So, is Google penalizing people for clicking on searches and veering off to websites? Are the websites acting as distractions and consequently losing us the sales? There’s gotta be an explanation as this ‘misfortune’ has brought me the highest returns since I launched.
Insights? Thoughts?

comments.author: Lou Backover
comments.text: I hate to say it (and this sort of scares me), but it sounds like you're already getting traffic to your site, but the website…

comments.author: Sean Flanagan
comments.text: What probably happened is your website outage forced customers to interact directly through your Google Business Profile (call

posts.author: Ulises Garcia
posts.title: Hi everyone, I could really use some guidance and mentorship
posts.content:
Hi everyone,
I could really use some guidance and mentorship. I’ve started my business with my mom, and while we’ve set up our Google Business Profile and a website, I feel stuck.
My Google profile strength is still showing yellow, my website feels like it’s missing something, and I haven’t been able to get many customers lately.
I don’t have much money to spend on ads or hiring someone, but I’m very driven to learn and get this business moving forward. I’ve been trying to do everything on my own, but it feels overwhelming, and I know there’s so much I don’t know.
If anyone here would be willing to take a look at my website or give me advice on what steps I should focus on first, I’d be so grateful. Even just pointing me in the right direction would mean a lot.
This business means everything to me and my mom, and I truly want to make it work. Thank you in advance to anyone who can lend a hand or some words of wisdom.

posts.author: Chris Hachez
posts.title: Hey everyone, I've been in this group for well over a year now but finally started transitionin
posts.content:
Hey everyone,
I've been in this group for well over a year now but finally started transitioning to BK the past few days setting up all settings and customizing things to my liking.
I wanted to make sure I was fully prepared prior to transitioning from my other software to BK.
I hoping i can gather some insights from some BK Veterans.
I currently run an hourly pricing model but really hoping to transitioning new clientele to Bed/Bath Sqft Model. I recently increased prices from $45 to $47 and I have some of my first clients which got increased to $40 (total of 5 clients).
For those, who have done the switch from hourly to a flat rate pricing structure. What advice or considerations do you have?
Side note, Roger F. Schulze I may need your assistant when you have a chance to go over my twilio connection and see if i did everything right. I would love your input if there's anything else you see off in the settings. I purchased a 1 Day BK course from Pioneering Clean so I hope I have it down but want to make sure everything is up and running smoothly before launch on the 1st.
Appreciate all the value this group brings! Shout out to Jacques H. Bastien

posts.author: Amanda Miller
posts.title: Happy Monday Spent the weekend at Uba (my off-grid homestead) and I'm still riding the energy f
posts.content:
Happy Monday
Spent the weekend at Uba (my off-grid homestead) and I'm still riding the energy from it.
Water system for the camper — started. First level of the shed — done. Found the perfect hammock spot and we're building a whole little kick-it area around it. And we cleaned up the land, which always makes everything feel more possible.
There's something about working with your hands on something you're building from nothing that resets your whole brain. No emails. No notifications. Just problems to solve and progress you can see.
This morning I'm heading out to do my monthly clean. I make a point to get out and do at least one cleaning job a month — not because I have to, but because I never want to lead from a place that's disconnected from what my team experiences every day. It's grounding in a way I didn't expect when I started doing it. That's what
360 Home Services is all about
.
Every day is what we make it. We have the power to shape our days and ultimately our lives.
Remember — "the most common way people give up their power is by thinking they don't have any."
Make this week yours.

posts.author: Carol Pacheco
posts.title: Question about dashboard visibility / provider status We currently use MaidPad, and one thing w
posts.content:
Question about dashboard visibility / provider status
We currently use MaidPad, and one thing we really like is how easy it is to see the job status directly on the schedule/dashboard.
For example:
* When the cleaner is On The Way, it shows a car icon.
* When the cleaner is at the customer’s house / checked in, it shows a house icon.
* When the job is completed, it shows a checkmark.
* When the job has not started yet, it simply shows the scheduled time.
Is there any way to do something similar in BookingKoala?
I’m looking for an easy visual way on the admin dashboard/schedule to quickly identify:
* Provider is On The Way
* Provider has arrived / checked in
* Cleaning has started
* Cleaning is completed
* Job has not started yet
Can BookingKoala show different icons, colors, status labels, or any other visual indicator on the dashboard/calendar for these provider status updates?
I want the office team to be able to look at the dashboard and immediately know what stage each cleaning is in without opening every booking one by one.
Has anyone set this up or found a good workaround?

comments.author: Gerry George Giles III
comments.text: I would like this feature as well.

posts.author: Jaclyn Fernandez
posts.title: Important Booking Notification Issue
posts.content:
Important Booking Notification Issue.. Please tell me there's a way to fix this..
When a client modifies their booking:
If they change details like square footage, bathroom count, etc. I do receive a email notification. But if they change the date or time , we receive NO notification at all. It only updates on the calendar.
This is a huge issue for us.
Last week, a client rescheduled their 8am cleaning at 1am, 7 hours notice. My cleaner didn’t check the app that morning (only the night before) and showed up at the usual scheduled time only to be turned away because the booking had been moved, and we did not receive any email notification, or even a notification IN bk.
Now, it’s happened again. a client canceled a booking at 11:30 PM for the next morning, and again…no notification. I just happened to catch it now as I am in BK so now I have to manually reach out to my cleaner due to this issue.
Has anyone found a way to get alerts for booking date/time changes? Any help appreciated!

comments.text: This is not in regards on how to fix the online automated notice issue. But just something I came across because I also had la

posts.author: Toussaint Louverture
posts.title: Feature request Support I just posted this feature request to BookingKoala request page and wou
posts.content:
Feature request Support
I just posted this feature request to BookingKoala request page and would really appreciate if you could go vote it up
I attached a poll on this facebook post for us to vote
BUt the one I believe will matter most if the one in this link :
https://feedback.bookingkoala.com/contribute/feature-requests/e/49215/
Feature Request
Title: Allow Providers to View Pay Summaries (Weekly, Monthly, Yearly) with Payment Details
Details:
Currently, providers can only view their pay on a per-job basis. It would be very helpful if they could also see summarized reports of their total earnings by week, month, and year.
In addition, the summary should show:
Which jobs have been paid vs. unpaid
The payment method used (e.g., direct deposit, PayPal, etc.)
The exact date when payments were made
This would give providers full visibility into their income, payment history, and outstanding balances, making it easier for them to manage their finances.

posts.author: Jayger McGough Tomasino
posts.title: The massive cloudflare outage has impacted thousands of companies(X, Claude, Shopify, AT&T, Pay
posts.content:
The massive cloudflare outage has impacted thousands of companies(X, Claude, Shopify, AT&T, Paypal, Uber etc)...
Until service is restored, here’s how you can stay on track today:
For Business Owners / Admin Teams: All booking details can be pulled from your email inbox, because BK sends email notifications with your booking info to the admin account and to staff accounts depending on your notification settings.
Search your inbox using keywords like:
“Cleaning Scheduled,” “New Booking,”
For Cleaners:
Tell your cleaners to check their email to get their booking details, every assigned cleaning includes:
• Date & time
• Client information
• Building/parking instructions
• Add-ons
• Notes left by clients

posts.title: We do not pay for lunch breaks
posts.content: We do not pay for lunch breaks. However when a staff clicks on “lunch” while at a booking, and then resumes after lunch, the total time card still calculates the whole time. We have done tests with all of the options: Approved - time reported with lunch break, Approved - time reported without lunch break, and Approved - time reported and travel time and it ends up the same total (total time) no matter what we select. We also have selected both yes and no to the screen prompt “Would you like to override the bookings estimate estimated time with the actual recorded time?” and it gives the same total on the export log no matter what we do. What settings would fix this?

posts.title: Hey BK Fam! Is anyone else running into this? A customer’s card fails, they go into the portal
posts.content:
Hey BK Fam!
Is anyone else running into this? A customer’s card fails, they go into the portal and add a new one, but they miss that tiny "Adjust all future appointments" checkbox.
The result: The new card only attaches to ONE booking. Next time their appointment rolls around, the system tries the old, dead card again.
It feels like a major friction point for customers. I’d love to see BK:
Make the toggle HUGE or add a pop-up asking if they want to update all future jobs.
Are you guys seeing this too? If enough of us tag the BK team or upvote this, maybe we can get a UI update to save us all some customer service headaches!

posts.author: Chris Schwab
posts.title: Okay, weird question
posts.content:
Okay, weird question: who the heck are all you people?
We've got 15,000+ BK members in here now and I don't actually know most of you. Which is insane.
So I'm stealing a game format I saw somewhere and making it cleaning-business themed.
Here's how it works: introduce yourself but make it interesting. Give us your name, where you're at, what you do, and then two truths and one lie about your cleaning business journey.
I'll go first so you can see what I mean:
Chris | Yokohama, Japan
Long-time remote cleaning business owner, now I coach others go remote and hire great VAs.
My two truths and a lie:
A client once asked if we could clean during their Zoom meetings because we're "that quiet" (I was so confused)
I did a sales call at 2am in my pajamas because a client could "only talk at 1pm EST" (timezones are hell)
I approved a $5K equipment purchase via voice message while hiking Mount Fuji (seemed like a good idea at the time?)
Guess which one is BS
Alright, your turn. Copy the format or just wing it, I don't care. The point is let's actually meet each other.
Throw yours in the comments. Try to guess other people's lies. Tag someone you've been wondering about but never talked to.
This should be fun!

posts.content:
*VACUUM POST*
Hi everyone, yes, I know not exactly the most riveting of topics! I would really love your insight into how in your company you handle vacuums among teams. As company owners and operators, what is expected from us? All our team are contractors. At the moment our company provides each commited worker a $300 pacvac. I often find myself running around buying new spare parts if one of them needs repairing. Takes time out of my day and we cover all costs. Is this normal? My husband thinks I do too much. I want them to have good-working, reliable, powerful vacuums but also find they may not look after them as much as they should as they count on the fact we just pay.
Many companies around us will ask all cleaners to provide their own vacuum. Would appreciate your advice and what is your usual practice.
Thank you!

posts.author: EmeraldLizard3545
posts.title: Just looking for some pre-legal advice
posts.content:
Just looking for some pre-legal advice:
I hired a cleaner as an independent contractor and she met all criteria. When I finally assign a job to her she acts completely different. She complained that it was too far, even though she had texted me multiple times asking for a job and took the job after I verbally told her what neighborhood it was. The address is also visible before accepting on BK.
She goes and tells me she will actually made an appointment that day so she has to leave and come back. After 2 hours of cleaning, she also is complaining that there are workers there and there's no water. How you've been cleaning without water for 2 hours and just now mention it was beyond me. I told her contractors would be there and only one area of the house didn't have water, the rest of the house had running water, flushing toilets, etc.
She cleaned a 4-BR house from 9am to 6pm or so and uploaded pictures of a half clean home and asked to get paid the same day, even though I pay weekly and told her this at multiple points, as well as our it in her agreement. I let her know the contractor over the job received complaints from the owners that the downstairs wasn't cleaned and there was embarrassing amounts of dust on the ceiling fans, shower pan uncleaned, etc.
She said she can return but not when she was asked.. She told me she was available on an agreed day-4 days later-but didn't want to come because she would be hung over from the night before and needed to get paid to come back or at least get gas money.
Flabbergasted, I told her if she didn't return I'd send another cleaner and split her pay. I ended up sending another cleaner and asked her to not return and I would just pay her and that would be her last job.
Do I have to pay her at all? I sent two other cleaners and want to split her pay 3 ways. Can I do that? Her contract also says she needs to provide insurance and never provided her liability insurance. She actually told me she would try and get some but didn't. I don't want to skip paying her, but I'd like to subtract the cost for the other cleaners from her pay. Can I do that legally? I just want to pay her and move on.

comments.author: Alina Kalinina
comments.text: I haven’t read your contract, so it’s hard to say exactly what you can or can’t do. But I’ve been in a very similar situation.

posts.author: Victor Jacon
posts.title: [PF Cleaning Adelaide - 5 months review] Hey everyone!
posts.content:
[PF Cleaning Adelaide - 5 months review]
Hey everyone! I thought it’d be interesting to share a bit about my journey as a small business owner.
I run PF Cleaning Adelaide, with my wife helping me manage things. For a while, it was just a glorified sole-trader business, but five months ago, I decided to go all in, hiring, training, advertising, and actually scaling it into a real business.
*Background Context*
I’ve always wanted to own a local business, but I wasn’t sure what to start.
I’ve worked as a marketing coordinator for nearly 7 years, so generating leads was easy. The challenge? Finding something I could learn from scratch, train others in, and eventually build into a business.
I’m not a plumber, electrician, carpenter, detailer, or dog groomer, so those were out. But back when I lived in Brazil, I made a decision: when I moved to Australia, I would work as a cleaner.
And that’s exactly what I did. Once I arrived, I landed a cleaning job and… turns out, I LOVE CLEANING!
I don’t know what’s wrong with me ahaha I genuinely enjoy it!
Anyway, I thought hiring people would be easy. I was so wrong.
Australia is facing a massive labor shortage, with fewer immigrants and students coming in. On top of that, most people don’t actually like cleaning, it’s just a job they accept because they need to pay the bills.
My biggest pain point has been finding decent teams to allow growth.
Please consider I will use Australian Dolars instead of USD.
*A quick snapshot of my current status*
25 jobs per week, approx $3913 per week, 7 cleaning teams, 37 new leads per week, 13 reviews on google, 4.8 stars average
*Investments*
Currently spending $150 per week on ADS, $120-$200 per week on a part time assistant, $90 per month on booking koala plan, insurance, hosting etc.. Nothing fancy.
I still can't make a living of the cleaning business, so I still work as a part-time marketing coordinator.
*Goals*
My current goals are to reach 40 jobs per week.
It’s been a wild five months, I have almost quit two times, and I’m excited to see where this goes. Thanks for reading!
if you’ve got any questions (or hiring tips
), drop them in the comments!

posts.author: SereneKoala1586
posts.title: We had recently had a odd grouping of fake one-star spam reviews
posts.content:
We had recently had a odd grouping of fake one-star spam reviews. None of these are customers of ours we have no record of any of these individuals there's no words behind the reviews just random brand new one star reviews with newer accounts or accounts that haven't been used in years.
We're trying to log into the review dispute tool but for some reason we continue to keep getting an error message which is giving us an inability to go in and dispute these reviews.
We have cleared the cache the history we've used multiple devices and still continue to keep getting the same error message.
Our Google my business profile is optimized active and working just fine but regardless of either email we used associated with this account what computer system we utilize we continue to keep getting the same error message which says:
Error
Sorry, something went wrong please contact support
You can also report reviews in Google search and Google maps
We can not seem to do anything about it currently. This has been going on for the last few days. We still have five fake one star reviews were unable to contest or flag that clearly has implications that are negative on our business but the much larger concern that we have outside of that is the fact that we have an inability to dispute them due to an error with the dispute tool.
I'm looking to see if someone has experienced this before and if there is a way to either get around this or to resolve this from your experience.
Alternatively if you've never experienced this before or you're unsure if you can at least potentially point me in the right direction because I've tried to utilize Google's contact us and email them but we always receive the same email and response which says we're unsure of what you need and then just gives us a bunch of links.
Google lately just seems like one big loop with no human interaction to assist real business owners and it's becoming increasingly more challenging to receive the assistance needed which is why I'm reaching out here in hopes that somebody would be able to assist us and getting this resolved. Thank you.

posts.author: Alina Kalinina
posts.title: Hey guys, I could really use some advice on this
posts.content:
Hey guys, I could really use some advice on this.
Earlier today I got a call from someone claiming they were affiliated with Google and could help with verification. My listing was recently flagged for re-verification after some updates, so I knew right away this was one of those scams and didn’t engage.
Then later the same day, I get a 1-star review.
At first glance it looks legit. It’s a Local Guide account with 80+ reviews and shows as someone local. But I am certain this is not an actual client.
The claims don’t match how we operate at all. They mention different cleaners and inconsistency, which isn’t how we run things. We keep the same provider for recurring clients unless the client asks for a change. I checked our records and we’ve never had a client with that name. No recent cancellations, no long-term unhappy clients. We’re still small enough that I know all of our recurring clients.
I even checked with my cleaners since the profile has photos, and none of them recognize this person or the home.
So it just doesn’t add up.
My concern is that this could turn into more fake reviews and then someone reaching out offering “help” to remove them.
Has anyone dealt with something like this before? What’s the best way to handle it and prevent it from escalating?
I already responded publicly and asked them to reach out so we can locate their booking, and I’m planning to report the review as well but since it’s a local guide, my chances are next to nothing.
Would really appreciate any advice.

comments.author: Carlyon Christian
comments.text: Unfortunately I have dealt with this several times in the past. Flagging it will not work. Your best bet is to respond to the

posts.author: Ami Felsino
posts.title: How do you handle customers demanding to speak to the owner when you built the business up so y
posts.content:
How do you handle customers demanding to speak to the owner when you built the business up so you wouldn't have to deal with customers first-hand anymore?
Please don't say suck it up and talk to the customer. I'm not interested in doing that. We all know at a certain point it doesn't make logical sense for the owner to be customer-facing.
For context, we messed up and caused damage to a client's hardwood floor. We explained to the customer that the VA handles all insurance claims and that talking to the owner wouldn't make sense as he's never filed an insurance claim and the VA would give better guidance for the process. The customer still demanded an "apology call" from the owner, despite the fact that we gave him an $100 refund even though insurance will fully compensate them for the damage.
I decided to not call the client because I've had a similar instance happened and it's usually just about them wanting to get a bigger refund than we already gave them. And then even after getting yelled at, the customer still wrote a 1-star review, hence me having the opinion that this kind of thing really isn't worth my time. And even if it was, I still wouldn't want to speak to the customer to be frank, because of my vision to not be customer-facing and to allocate those tasks to VAs.
Thanks in advance.

How many of you send a link to complete the booking instead of taking card info over the phone? We do most of ours by phone, and even though we explain our cancellation and rescheduling policy every time, we still get people upset about the fees.
We had one cancel a $600 job while our contractors were already on the way because they “found someone cheaper.” We charged the full amount and still paid our contractors since they rearranged their schedules for us. The client disputed the charge, and even though we had proof of the last-minute cancellation, the policy on our website, and booking transcripts, we still lost.
Now I’ve got another cancellation fee dispute. It feels like no matter how careful we are, people can get away with not paying just by disputing. Do you think making people book online (with the checkbox) is better than handling it over the phone? My only worry is losing bookings if we don’t grab card details right away.

posts.title: STRIPE DISPUTE
posts.content:
STRIPE DISPUTE: FAILS
We don't get these often, but when we do - we lose - who has had success in the dispute process?
Details:
Majority of the disputes have been based on our NO-SHOW CANCELLATION FEES - customers are marking it as 'service not provided' -
Communications:
-Booking form has the cancellation policy written before users submit their payment details.
- Booking form checkbox accepting terms and conditions, also evident
-confirmation email (cancellation policy written)
-Booking SMS text reminder (policy reiterated)
Evidence provided to Stripe:
-We have text messages, outbound calls (all time stamped), and photos of our team physically on-site
Conclusion: Dispute lost.
Keen to hear what others have done to address this - We can't imagine how much more transparent and communicative, our cancellation policy can be.

posts.author: Ryan Knoll
posts.title: BK Fam! TL;DR: BookingKoala needs better reporting, around recurring clients, cancellations vs
posts.content:
BK Fam!
TL;DR: BookingKoala needs better reporting, around recurring clients, cancellations vs. skips, technician performance, customer scorecards, and capacity utilization, so we can make better decisions on hiring, pricing, and retention. Let's get some community love for getting these things added. Use this link to show support > https://feedback.bookingkoala.com/contribute/feature-requests/e/49793/
Sharing a few reporting features that would make a huge difference for scaling companies and massively improve BK’s reporting.
I would love community support in pushing for better reporting.
The bigger a company gets, the bigger these reporting gaps feel.
I've tried building a lot of these with external tools, but they always seem janky or lack the data that BK has.
The biggest reporting gaps are in recurring revenue, retention, and team performance.
1) RECURRING CUSTOMER REPORTS
These would be incredibly valuable for tracking business health and growth.
Would love to see reporting for:
Number of active recurring clients
Example: “We have 250 active recurring clients”
Recurring churn
How many recurring clients canceled this month
New recurring clients per month
Example: “We got 16 new recurring clients this month”
MRR from recurring clients
One-time to recurring conversion (ascension)
Example: “Of 40 one-time clients, 12 became recurring this month”
For service businesses, this is the heartbeat of growth.
2) CANCELLATIONS REPORTING (IMPORTANT: CANCELS VS SKIPS)
A big one for us is separating cancels vs skips.
Right now, a skipped appointment and a true cancellation can show up similarly in BK, but they mean very different things when you’re looking at retention.
It would be super helpful to track/report these separately:
Skipped appointments
Canceled service / lost customer
That distinction matters a lot when diagnosing churn.
3) TECHNICIAN-LEVEL PERFORMANCE REPORTS
It would be amazing to have technician-specific reports nested inside each technician profile (ideally near their rating/performance section).
Suggested metrics:
Number of recurring customers actively assigned
Customer churn rate
How many of their assigned customers canceled or skipped this month
Review/feedback rate
Not just rating score, but how many customers leave feedback
In our experience, review frequency is a strong signal of technician performance
Example: great cleaners may get ~50% review rate, while weaker performers may be closer to ~10%
This would make coaching and performance management much easier.
4) CUSTOMER-LEVEL REPORTS / CUSTOMER SCORECARD
A customer profile report would also be really useful for retention and account management.
Suggested metrics:
Completed bookings
Total spend
Number of cancellations
Super helpful because a small percentage of customers may cancel frequently and create scheduling/ops issues
This would help identify top customers and problem patterns faster.
5) CAPACITY REPORTING (HUGE METRIC)
This one is a big deal for us:
Capacity / % of available spots sold (booked)
This has been a HUGE metric in our business.
When we get above ~90% capacity, we know we’re generally safe to:
raise prices, and/or
hire more cleaners
It’s honestly a much more useful operational metric than revenue alone, since revenue can move up/down for lots of reasons.
WHY THIS MATTERS
Reporting features like this would help operators:
better track recurring revenue growth
measure retention/churn more accurately
coach technicians using real performance indicators
identify high-value customers and recurring problem patterns faster
make better hiring/pricing decisions based on capacity
Appreciate everything BookingKoala has built. They've been a big part of our growth, and better reporting would help many of us grow faster.
What can we do as a community to push for better reporting?

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: Client Acquisition vs Client Retention I've seen that most cleaning business owners stuck under
posts.content:
Client Acquisition vs Client Retention
I've seen that most cleaning business owners stuck under $10k/mo are usually blind to a crucial part of every business...
When someone can't grow past that point - it’s usually one of these two problems:
Client Acquisition or Client Retention
1) Client Acquisition (getting new customers)
This is marketing + sales + systems.
Marketing: Are you consistently getting inbound leads every week?
Sales: Do you know how to convert those leads into booked jobs (fast response time, confident pricing, objection handling, solid follow-up)?
Systems: Do you have a simple process so you’re not reinventing the wheel every time a lead comes in (scripts, CRM, automations, pipeline, reminders)?
If any one of those is weak, you’ll feel like you’re “working so hard” but still stuck under $5k/mo, relying on referrals, or having random good weeks followed by dead weeks.
And honestly… this is where I see most people suffering. Many people can't even get past $5k because of client acquisition problems.
Not because they’re lazy, just nobody ever taught them a real, repeatable acquisition machine...
Instead, biz owners that I connect with are saying they were taught:
“Just post on Facebook and hope it works”
“Run some ads” (with no idea what to say or how to track it)
“Referrals are the best"
“Just do great work and people will magically keep coming”
“Lower your prices with discount to get the sale”
No wonder it feels frustrating!!!
That’s not a long-term, profitable, scalable strategy...
The worse part is that when you've been taught this - it makes you feel like you’re doing everything “right” while still getting inconsistent results.
What's the other part of this business??
2) Client Retention (keeping customers)
Retention is service delivery + communication + consistency.
Doing the basics extremely well, every time
Setting expectations clearly (so you don’t create “surprise disappointment”)
Having a simple system for issues (fix it fast, make it right, move on)
Staying top-of-mind (easy rebooking, check-ins, little touches)
Usually, this is where people are already doing well! They have cleaners that do great (or they ARE a great cleaner), but they can't scale.
Basically:
Client Acquisition = how you build momentum.
Client Retention = how you build stability.
If acquisition is weak, retention can’t save you.
If retention is weak, acquisition gets expensive and exhausting.
So if you want the simple version of this business:
Get leads → close leads → deliver well → keep clients.
If you’ve been feeling stuck, I’d bet money it’s on the client acquisition side.
Don't mind my oversimplification for teaching purposes!

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: 13 months in business… this was March last year vs March this year
posts.content:
13 months in business… this was March last year vs March this year.
Last year (March 2025):
Revenue: $7,005
Net Profit: -$1,890
This year (March 2026):
Revenue: $29,607
Net Profit: $10,646
(and yeah, some of that top line is just sales tax passing through — so the actual growth feels even more meaningful when you look at it that way)
Same business. Same market. Same us.
Just 13 months apart.
When we started last February, life honestly looked like this:
• Constant stress about money
• Saying yes to every job (even the bad ones)
• Doing everything ourselves — cleaning, quoting, scheduling, fixing mistakes
• No systems, just reacting all day
• Wondering if this would even work
It didn’t feel like a “business.”
It felt like survival.
Now it looks a lot different:
• Systems handle most of the day-to-day... We spend less than 2 hours a week on this business (we have 2 other businesses we focus on in real estate now)
• 1x full-time VA and 1x part-time VA is fulfilling the work
• We spend more time on leadership (directing VAs on quarterly goals) and improving things based on weekly "mistakes made" reports and KPIs
We still work hard! Just in a very different way than before.
A few of the biggest things we’ve learned along the way:
1. Systems matter more than effort
Grinding harder didn’t fix much! Building repeatable processes did.
2. Speed to lead + systemic sales follow-up is huge
A lot of growth came from just being faster, persistent, and more consistent than others.
3. You don’t need clarity to move forward
We were unsure a lot of the time! We just kept going anyway.
4. Profit changes everything
Going from negative to solidly positive shifts how the whole business FEELS on a day-to-day. We are often emotional before we are logical. Entrepreneurship has a sneaky way of improving you as a person so that your business also improves.
5. BK and other cleaning biz FB groups has helped more than I expected
A lot of small tweaks we made came from posts and comments in here. Special shoutout to the eye-opening conversations I had this past year with Michael Zielinski, Jacob Elijah, Jacques H. Bastien, and many others.
Just wanted to share this for anyone in the early stages right now.
It can feel messy for a while… but it does start to click - and it IS worth it to "be your own boss". I'm enjoying the mini-vacations and freedom to step away from work and not worry about paying bills.
If you have any questions about anything we did or what changed, I’m happy to share! At this point, it feels more fulfilling to see other people win than anything else.
God bless!

comments.author: Susan Martin Mizialko
comments.text: Congrats!! Hard work pays off....You should be very proud! Over 35% net at 30k a month is my goal to retire from my w2!!! I wo

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: Progress report time… this month is our best ever Every so often I like sharing the biggest les
posts.content:
Progress report time… this month is our best ever
Every so often I like sharing the biggest lessons since we started from nothing 10 months ago.
• July: ~$10k
• Last month: ~$19k
• This month (projected): ~$24k
Monthly revenue has doubled since the podcast I did with Michael Zielinski in July.
Here are my (updated) biggest lessons so far — hope they help you grow faster than we have:
1. “Your net worth is your network” (cringe but true)
You will save time and money by connecting with people who are ahead of you.
This group is full of amazing people — use it. Don’t be scared to ask for help.
2. Optimize… but know what you’re optimizing for
Most people optimize for profit.
One of my friends does $50k/mo… but works himself to the bone every day, 7 am to 8 pm. He optimizes for PROFIT.
I optimize for:
• LONG-TERM GROWTH (hence our track record so far)
• PASSIVE INCOME (I played more disc golf than I worked last week)
If I was optimizing for PROFIT, I’d be doing things very differently.
Whatever you optimize for should fit your lifestyle and goals.
My big goal right now is to earn equity in an alternative investment fund I’m doing marketing and sales consulting for. So ~90% of my time goes there, and my cleaning biz gets 2–3 hours a week.
3. There ARE silver bullets
I’ve had multiple changes completely transform the business in 24 hours. Objection Handling, SEO, PPC, Attribution…
If things feel way harder than they should, you might be missing something foundational.
4. Business is better with friends
I don’t think I’ll go into another venture alone again.
I love working with people, I feed off conversation, and who better to get rich with than a good buddy?
5. Borrow other people’s expertise
I ask my grandma how to bake.
I ask my coach if my technique is right.
I ask my cleaners how to clean.
You don’t need to know everything about the cleaning business.
But you should know someone who knows more than you and be willing to lean on them. This community is GOLDEN and FREE. USE IT!!!
6. You win by not quitting
Most people quit.
Even if you grow slowly, if you stay committed to this for two years, you’ll be in a great place.
⸻
That’s all I’ve got for now. I wish I had understood these earlier — hope they help you grow faster than I did

posts.author: Mark Baker
posts.title: Postpone Feature – Does Anyone Actually Use It?
posts.content:
Postpone Feature – Does Anyone Actually Use It?
We turned off the Postpone feature a while back, but I see BK has made a lot of updates to the cancellation and reschedule settings.
I’m curious if anyone here is actively using Postpone with customers.
Does it get used much, and how does it work out in practice?
WHY POSTPONE SOUNDS GOOD
Customer convenience – Customers can pause their recurring series instead of cancelling. It’s simple and feels less permanent. Perfect for vacations, extended travel, or snowbirds (we are in Canada) who plan to restart later.
Retention – The booking relationship stays alive. Since they don’t need to rebook from scratch, they’re more likely to come back.
Slot protection – If postpone locks their slot, their recurring time is still waiting for them. They don’t risk losing their day/time to someone else.
Reduced cancellations – Some customers who would normally cancel entirely might instead postpone. That helps keep long-term retention numbers healthier.
Flow – Because the series is preserved, reminders still go out before the booking resumes. Customers don’t fall through the cracks.
WHY I’M CAUTIOUS
Blocked recurring slots – A postponed slot is reserved and can’t be sold to another recurring customer. At best, you can fill it with one-time bookings, but that’s patchwork revenue and takes extra effort.
Lost recurring revenue opportunities – If a slot is tied up for months, you miss the chance to put in a new recurring customer who would deliver predictable revenue week after week. When the snowbird comes back, they can always get back in line.
Unlimited postponements – Without limits, a customer could keep pushing it forward and essentially hold a slot without paying.
SO, I’D LOVE TO HEAR FROM OTHERS:
Do you let your customers use Postpone?
Has it helped you retain more customers, or do you find it hurts recurring revenue?
Would limits (like how far ahead they can postpone, or how many times they can do it) make it more practical?

posts.title: Has anyone here registered their LLC as a foreign entity in another state and then filed for an
posts.content:
Has anyone here registered their LLC as a foreign entity in another state and then filed for an alternate name?
My LLC name includes my home state in it (Colorado). When I opened a second location in New Jersey I filed for an alternate name there instead. Now my Google Business Profile got suspended in NJ because of this.
The reinstatement company says they need a business document that shows both my New Jersey name and New Jersey address. The problem is that because it is an alternate name there is not a single document that has both.
Here is what I currently have:
Certificate of Alternate Name which shows that my Colorado LLC operates under a different name in NJ but it does not list an address
Certificate of Authority which shows my Colorado LLC name and my Colorado business address and I cannot change the name on this
NJ Registration Certificate which shows my Colorado LLC name with my NJ registered agent
We tried sending paired documents but Google will not accept that. At this point I am not sure what to send and it almost feels like having an alternate name prevents me from having a GBP in New Jersey.
Has anyone dealt with this before? How did you handle the documentation so Google would reinstate your profile?

posts.author: Steve Tam
posts.title: Hey everyone
posts.content: Hey everyone.  I realize a lot of companies here use contractors because the cost of W-2 is prohibitive (supplies, equipment, payroll taxes, workers comp, etc.).  But when I called insurance companies, Hiscox and Next, they both don't cover subcontractors.  Let me explain.  If you have coverage with Next, if subcontractors have an incident that requires an insurance claim, their insurance must cover first.  If the subcontractor does not have insurance, you broke their policy and Next will not cover you at all.  So they are the back-up insurance policy.  For Hiscox, their insurance policy covers your company.  It will not cover contractors work at all.  So if a contractor has an incident and the owners goes after them and they don't/can't pay, the owner goes after you, Hiscox will not pay.  The subcontractor must have insurance that covers their work.  Hiscox will only cover work done by your company and it's employees (W-2).  But a lot of subcontractors don't buy insurance because they see themselves as solo cleaners and don't think about insurance coverage.  Or do you guys have a policy that contractors MUST buy insurance or you will not give them work.

posts.author: Silvia Mar
posts.title: Sometimes we look at other people’s results and think we’re behind, not good enough, or that th
posts.content:
Sometimes we look at other people’s results and think we’re behind, not good enough, or that this path isn’t for us. I want to share my story to remind us of something important: we don’t all start from the same place, and that doesn’t make us less.
I started with $0.
Literally with the vacuum from my house, cleaning by myself for $25 an hour, with no business knowledge at all.
I come from the world of cleaners, and that felt like my destiny—clean forever, work hard, and settle.
But I refused to believe that was all I could be. I wanted more… and I still do.
English is my second language, and honestly, I still struggle to feel confident answering calls or doing cold calls. I make mistakes, I stumble, I doubt myself—and I keep going anyway.
It took me a full year to understand BookingKoala,
another year to learn Google Ads, and I’m still struggling with it.
Building and implementing my own system has taken me 4 years of trial, error, and starting over.
I also rebuilt my website three different times with three different developers, and I’m now on the fourth attempt, still in progress. I’ve lost time, money, and patience—but I’ve gained something incredibly valuable: clarity. I now know exactly what I want and what I won’t settle for.
Along the way, I also hurt my back cleaning. As hard as that was, it became one of my greatest teachers. Having done the physical work myself gave me a deep understanding of what this job truly takes.
That experience is what allows me today to understand, respect, and build the right team. I don’t lead from an office—I lead from lived experience.
Through all of this, I’ve learned something critical: having the right people on your team is essential. Not everyone adds value, and choosing the wrong people can be expensive.
Failure costs time and money—but it’s also part of learning. Every mistake has made me clearer, stronger, and more intentional.
Today, my business is more than revenue—it’s my ikigai.
I hire women, single mothers, heads of household. Women who, like me, are working to move forward and create a better life.
I truly feel that I’m contributing something meaningful to the world by creating real opportunities from a place I deeply understand and respect.
Yes, sometimes I compare myself.
Sometimes I see people making double or more than I have in less time, and it can be discouraging. I’m human.
But then I remind myself:
my starting point was different.
My platform was different.
My path didn’t come with advantages.
So I choose to celebrate myself.
Celebrate that I’m still here.
Celebrate that I didn’t quit.
Celebrate that every day I feel a little more confident as a business owner and as a person.
I truly love this group because I’ve met amazing allies here—real people who share openly and make this journey feel less lonely. Thank you from the bottom of my heart.
If you’re starting from the bottom, feeling scared or unsure:
your process matters.
Your growth counts.
And continuing forward—even with fear—is already a win

posts.author: Kirsten Boudreaux
posts.title: Here’s the secret to not wasting money on paid ads
posts.content:
Here’s the secret to not wasting money on paid ads: (There’s no reason to be afraid of them)
Find out who your customer segment is in your market. Here’s a quick how to.
If you have clients collect information on who they are and make a list. Find out their age-range, average salary for their zip code, other details such as if they are married, have kids, retired, ect…
Bonus: Find the chillest clients you have and interview them. Ask them why they booked with you to begin with, what value they get out of your service, what they love and hate about your company, and if they have hired other services in the past. Formulate your set of questions and perform several “customer discovery” interviews. Make sure you take the time to listen more than you speak and tell them to be as honest a possible. Mentally prepare yourself to hear negative feedback but trust that this is going to help you out so much in the long run. Bonus points if you offer something in return such as a Starbucks gift card, it doesn’t have to be related to your service make it novel.
I’ve done this before and I have several OG clients I can rely on for honest feedback and helpful information. It pays to develop these relationships trust me!
Ok once you have data create your first at set. For demographics target behaviors that the clients you interviewed had such as income level, hobbies, interests, ect… At this stage you’re going to start to see a trend.
Run an ad set for about a week. You may get bookings but this isn’t the goal at this stage. The goal is to gather more data so you can make better decisions when investing your advertising dollars.
Ok so now we have arrived at the fun part. You have concrete data and hopefully some conversions. Look to see what the trends are overall. Don’t be afraid to be surprised. When I started my company I assumed that my target market was younger busy professionals and parents. I was shocked when the data showed that it was actually women 55+.
You can stop here or, perform some additionally customer discovery interviews, which I did.
I found every person I knew in my area who was a woman 55+ and interviewed them based on what their pain points were with cleaning.
I was shocked to hear that they often struggled to keep a clean home due to physical limitations such as back, and knee issues and time constraints. They told me that they host at their home and often want to free up their time to spend more time with their grandkids and host events at their homes. Many of them are still in the workforce but often have a higher income at their age and can finally justify (without guilt) hiring a service.
Now you can refine your copy and demographics to match EXACTLY who your business targets. Remember a dollar invested in customer discovery and testing is NEVER a dollar wasted.
Good luck!

posts.author: Adam Belko
posts.title: For those who have been successfully running cleaning businesses, how do you retain your cleane
posts.content:
For those who have been successfully running cleaning businesses, how do you retain your cleaners?
Here in Australia, most trade jobs I’ve done had the same issue - people who don’t care much about the work, take shortcuts, lack motivation, and aren’t interested in improving, yet complain about low pay. Now that I’m running a small cleaning business and planning to hire my first cleaner early next year, I’m wondering how do you actually keep people? And realistically, how long does a cleaner usually stay?
I feel like a big challenge is that cleaning is relatively low-skill work, so it doesn’t always attract ambitious people or those who think independently and can do tasks without me not checking everything after them. Obviously hiring process is super important to filter out those kind of people, but again, I feel like it’s hard to attract A players when margins are not so great in cleaning business.
Some ideas I’ve been considering for staff retention:
• Clear financial incentives — gradually increase pay as their responsibilities grow, and provide a path for career progression
• Team-building activities so they feel part of something more than just a shift, this would also strengthen everyone’s relationship
• Small gestures like shouting them a coffee occasionally to show appreciation
• Performance bonuses to recognise high-quality work and reliability
Would love to hear what has actually worked for others. What keeps your stuff happy, motivated, and reliable?

This is basically my entire marketing philosophy distilled in 15 mins.
Here's my exact ads + setup in Meta... Plus showing you an oil and gas investment company spending $70k+/mo in Meta ads using the SAME philosophy...
You can literally copy paste what I have here by googling "Meta Ads Library" into your own marketing.
The two Ai's I am referring to are Manus Ai and ArcAds Ai...
The books I refer to are 1 Million Followers by Brendan Kane, Expert Secrets by Russell Brunson, and Alex Hormozi's $100m series.
You DO NOT need to pay an agency or anyone else to do marketing for you, it's best for you to learn how to do this yourself so you can grow your skillset as an entrepreneur.
I am not kidding when I say that there are courses and gurus out there that would charge $5k+ for this. I paid for an expensive course and learned the lesson so you don't have to.
Hope this helps, cheers to being on track for $35-40k this month. We started a little over a year ago from SCRATCH...
Shoutout Jacques H. Bastien for inspiring me and Jacob Elijah for challenging me.
Michael Zielinski I'm ready for podcast #2 bro LETS GET IT!!!
Seriously, this group has changed my life; thank those of you who take the time to help others here... Here to do my part

posts.author: Brian Powell
posts.title: We can all take a lesson from Joe Girard
posts.content:
We can all take a lesson from Joe Girard.
Who the heck is Joe Girard?
He’s in the Guinness Book of World Records as the greatest car salesman of all time.
In the morning when the dealership opened, there would be a line of people waiting to talk to Joe about buying a car. He sold an average of 6 cars a day. Nowadays, nobody even comes close to that.
His success was not due to him being an aggressive salesman, but rather one simple strategy.
Joe sent out nearly 13,000 greeting cards to his customers every month. He made sure everyone got a card from him, and when it got to be too much work he hired two full time assistants just to send cards. He would celebrate major holidays, anniversaries, birthdays, etc. With Joe’s card coming every month, his clients considered him part of the family.
They always got the impression that Joe cared and that made all the difference.
This simple act inspired loyalty and motivated customers to refer their friends to him.
This is how Joe did it. No matter what business you’re in, you can use the same simple strategy to always be top of mind with your clients.
So why aren’t you already sending cards on a regular basis? It’s probably because of the hassle involved with sending cards.
We now have technology that now makes this effortless. You can hop online, push a few buttons, and send a real card in the mail from 1 to thousands.
I’d love to connect and show you how you can do this.

posts.author: Devyn Hildebrandt
posts.title: I got super lucky and hired a rockstar Office Manager almost a year ago
posts.content:
I got super lucky and hired a rockstar Office Manager almost a year ago. As we’ve grown, finding a second person just as good has been really challenging. We went through three people before deciding to go back to just one for a few months.
Now, as busy season kicks off here in Florida, we finally brought on another #2 — and WOW, she’s amazing. It’s wild how big the gap is between a high performer and a low performer. Honestly, I don’t know if you can always spot it during interviews — sometimes you just have to bring them on and move quickly if it’s not a fit.
Not sure if using an agency would’ve made this process easier, but I’m grateful we stuck it out.
For those of you who’ve hired office staff or admins — what’s been your best strategy for finding great people?
Agencies, referrals, job boards, trial periods? Always curious what’s working for other cleaning business owners.

I started just before Christmas with help from a friend who runs a cleaning business in Canada. We just turned over £1000 this month in the South of England!
Just wanted to share as this was a target for me, and my cleaning business is definitely growing at a steady rate!
Keep going everyone!

I did have 3 cleaners on board but were not always reliable, so just kept the one on and it was a great move.
Was worth taking a step back to take a few steps forward, now I feel like I can take my time finding a second hard working cleaner to expand the team!
Good luck everyone.

posts.author: Alex Swetnam
posts.title: Feeling blessed We’ll start next month just shy of $10k/mo in recurring
posts.content:
Feeling blessed
We’ll start next month just shy of $10k/mo in recurring. Feel pretty awesome about getting here in 6 months (we started in Feb)!!
Biggest lesson for me has honestly been to lock in on one thing. All of my life I’ve been a serial entrepreneur, jumping from one thing to another; trying to juggle 2-3 projects at once.
This time, I decided to let go of two passion projects of mine; and live pretty broke in the beginning of the business so I could pick up some cleanings and learn the ins & outs of this business.
Now we’re growing steadily every month with a team of awesome cleaners and clients!
Time to leave the business behind, and start my coaching program for $10k…. right?!
